coredata.dc:title: Hybrid cross-linking for the synthesis of highly tough fluorescent alginate fibers
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dongnan
Zhang, Hong
Tang, Song
Bai, Zijian
Jiang, Jianyu
Wang, Yao
Yu, Yue
Quan, Fengyu
coredata.dc:description: The limited mechanical performance and functional constraints of alginate fibers significantly impede their development in the context of professional research. To address the aforementioned issues, this study initially utilized polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CD) with surface amino functionalization. Subsequently, in a sodium alginate (SA) solution, CD and vinyl silicon-based nanoparticles (VSNP) were co-introduced. Utilizing the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in polysaccharides, stable interactions were achieved with CD and VSNP. Incorporating a dynamic hydrogen-bonded crosslinking network among CD, VSNP and SA, the establishment of a calcium alginate (CA) ionically crosslinked network is facilitated via calcium-sodium ion exchange. Additionally, the interwoven and interlocked structure between the aforementioned dual networks was established. Employing a wet spinning process, the synergistic multiple dynamic energy dissipation mechanisms significantly enhance the mechanical performance. Fluorescent alginate fibers (CA/VSNP/CD) were prepared. The tensile strength was 3.81 cN/dtex, and the elongation at break was 13.16%. Compared to pure CA fibers, there were respective increases of 95.38% and 82.02%. Simultaneously, the fibers exhibit excitation-dependent emission characteristics. This work lays the foundation for potential applications of fluorescent alginate fibers in environmental monitoring, chemical sensors, dynamic anti-counterfeiting, and biomedical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Multifunctional composites
A. polymer-matrix composites (PMCs)
B. Mechanical properties
B. Synergism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Molecular structural characteristics of VSNP and optical properties of CD
Analysis of the strength, hydrophilicity and thermal properties of CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Research Project of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Key Research Project of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (grant numbers: LJKZZ20220073 ), State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fiber and Eco-Textiles ( Qingdao University ) (grant numbers: KFKT202224 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart of the preparation of VSNP and CD (a) and CA/VSNP/CD fibers (b).
FTIR spectra of VTES and VSNP (a), and CD (b); (c) UV–Vis spectrum of CD (images of CD solution under natural light (c1) and UV light (c2)); (d) fluorescence spectrum of CD.

(a1) Surface SEM image of CA fibers, (a2, a3) Cross-section SEM images of CA fibers; (b1) Surface SEM image of CA/VSNP/CD fibers, (b2, b3) Cross-section SEM images of CA/VSNP/CD fibers; EDS images of Ca (c1), Si (c2) and N (c3) on the cross-section of CA/VSNP/CD fibers.
FTIR(a)、XRD (b) and XPS(c) spectra of CA fibers and CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers; Infrared peak-fitting spectra of CA fibers (d) and CA/VSNP/CD fibers (e).

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of SA/VSNP/CD spinning solution; (b) The fluorescence spectrum of CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers (images of the freshly immersed fiber pieces in water (b1) and after soaking for 3 h (b2) under ultraviolet light); (c) the fluorescence lifetime of CA/VSNP/CD fibers; The microscopy images of CA/VSNP/CD fibers in bright field (d), fluorescence DAPI channel (e), and fluorescence GFP channel (f).
Tensile strength and elongation at break of CA fibers and CA/VSNP/CD fibers (a); Water contact angle images of CA fibers (left) and CA/VSNP/CD fibers (right) (b); TG and DTG images (c、d).
Literature summary and comparison of mechanical properties of alginate fibers.

head.ce:title: Hybrid cross-linking for the synthesis of highly tough fluorescent alginate fibers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textile and Material Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, Liaoning, 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Textile and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Textile and Material Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, Liaoning, 116034, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, Qingdao, Shandong, 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, Qingdao, Shandong, 266071, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The limited mechanical performance and functional constraints of alginate fibers significantly impede their development in the context of professional research. To address the aforementioned issues, this study initially utilized polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CD) with surface amino functionalization. Subsequently, in a sodium alginate (SA) solution, CD and vinyl silicon-based nanoparticles (VSNP) were co-introduced. Utilizing the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in polysaccharides, stable interactions were achieved with CD and VSNP. Incorporating a dynamic hydrogen-bonded crosslinking network among CD, VSNP and SA, the establishment of a calcium alginate (CA) ionically crosslinked network is facilitated via calcium-sodium ion exchange. Additionally, the interwoven and interlocked structure between the aforementioned dual networks was established. Employing a wet spinning process, the synergistic multiple dynamic energy dissipation mechanisms significantly enhance the mechanical performance. Fluorescent alginate fibers (CA/VSNP/CD) were prepared. The tensile strength was 3.81 cN/dtex, and the elongation at break was 13.16%. Compared to pure CA fibers, there were respective increases of 95.38% and 82.02%. Simultaneously, the fibers exhibit excitation-dependent emission characteristics. This work lays the foundation for potential applications of fluorescent alginate fibers in environmental monitoring, chemical sensors, dynamic anti-counterfeiting, and biomedical fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: China possesses abundant marine resources, encompassing a broad and diverse array of energy-rich sources. As a natural polysaccharide, sodium alginate(SA) serves as a type of cellulose. It has garnered remarkable biocompatibility [1], natural degradability [2], non-toxicity [3], and cost-effectiveness, among other advantages. It is extensively utilized in various domains, including textiles [4], flame-retardant materials [4,5], biomedical applications [6], and anti-counterfeiting measures [7], making it a highly sought-after material in marine resources. However, SA is a type of large molecular chain with a rigid structure, and it exhibits strong hydrogen bonding both within its molecules and between its segments. This limits the molecular chain's mobility [8,9], affecting its processing, mechanical properties, and functionalization. Therefore, there is an urgent need for in-depth research to develop alginate fibers with improved overall performance.

In recent years, there has been widespread attention on the fluorescent functionalization research of alginate fibers. Fluorescent fibers are a type of fiber material with fluorescent properties [3,10]. Its most notable and practical feature lies in its ability to emit distinctive colors under the illumination of specific wavelengths of excitation light. Once the specific light source is removed, the fiber can promptly revert to its original color. Their unique fluorescent characteristics make them highly advantageous for a multitude of applications, notably in domains like anti-counterfeiting measures and diverse fields. However, the majority of fluorescent fibers are prepared by incorporating fluorescent components through a blending method [11]. However, this approach often affects the dispersion of the blending system and the durability and processing continuity of the fluorescent fibers. For example, Simin [12]and others utilized electrospinning and Schiff base interactions to prepare fluorescent nanofibers of polyvinyl alcohol/oxidized sodium alginate (PVA-OSA) combined with carbon dots (CDs). These fibers exhibited excellent antioxidant properties and could be used as wound dressings for healing. However, their fluorescent durability was less than ideal. Furthermore, electrospun fibers have limitations in terms of mechanical performance and small-scale production [13], which restrict their practical applications. Song [7]and colleagues successfully prepared algae-based fibers (PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibers) using a wet spinning process with polyethyleneimine-modified carbonized polymer dots (PEI-CPDs). This provided a reliable design method for multiple anti-counterfeiting measures and fluorescent sensors. Compared to techniques like electrospinning, wet spinning allows for continuous spinning, achieving higher production yields and efficiency [14,15]. However, the tensile strength of these fibers was 2.14 cN/dtex.

Although research on the fluorescent functionalization modification of alginate fibers has yielded a series of achievements, one significant drawback of sodium alginate fibers has been largely overlooked: the traditionally low mechanical performance [16–18]. This has inspired researchers to develop fluorescent alginate fibers with enhanced toughness, aiming to expand their potential applications and value.

In this study, polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid were chosen as raw materials to obtain surface-aminated carbon quantum dots (CD) [19]. Simultaneously, vinyl silicon-based nanoparticles (VSNP) were introduced into the system. By employing a solution of calcium chloride (CaCl2) as the coagulation bath in the dynamic shaping process of wet spinning. Utilizing the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in algal polysaccharides, as well as the abundant –OH and –NH2 groups on the surface of VSNP and CD were used to construct dynamic cross-linking, and calcium-sodium ion exchange was utilized to obtain ionically cross-linked calcium alginate (CA), forming a double-network interlocking structure. The synergistic dynamic energy dissipation effect of multiple crosslinking significantly improved the mechanical properties, resulting in hybrid cross-linking for the synthesis of highly tough fluorescent alginate fibers (CA/VSNP/CD). These fibers have tremendous application potential in various fields, including environmental monitoring [20], ion adsorption [21], fluorescence sensors [22–24], information security encryption [25,26], and biomedical applications [27,28].

Sodium alginate (SA, (C6H7O6Na)n, Mw = 8.6 × 106 g/mol and M/G ratio of 5.7) [29], Food grade, was purchased from Qingdao Ming Yue Seaweed Group Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Obtained from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., the vinyltriethoxysilane (VTES) employed in this study was of analytical purity. The citric acid utilized in this study, sourced from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., was of analytical purity. Acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., the polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 1800) employed in this study was of analytical purity. Acquired from Tianjin KeMiOu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., both acetic acid and calcium chloride (CaCl2) utilized in this study were of analytical purity. Acquired from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd., the anhydrous ethanol utilized in this study was of analytical purity. The water employed in the experiments comprises distilled water from Watsons and deionized water.

As shown in Fig. 1 a. Initially, the ethanol and deionized water were blended in a mass ratio of 1:1, followed by dilution of a specific quantity of VTES with this mixture. Subsequently, the pH of the above solution was regulated to a range of 3.5–5.5 through the incorporation of acetic acid. Ultimately, the solution obtained underwent magnetic stirring at room temperature for 40 min until attaining a state of clarity and transparency, resulting in the formation of the hydrolyzed product, VSNP.

Introduce 0.1 g of citric acid and 0.92 mL of PEI into 30 mL of distilled water. Achieve complete dissolution through magnetic stirring for 1 h. Introduce the dissolved mixture into a 100 mL stainless steel hydrothermal reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. Simultaneously, the oven temperature was set to 180 °C and maintained for a duration of 30 min. Following this, the reactants within the hydrothermal reactor were subjected to a reaction period of 20 h in an oven. Following the completion of the hydrothermal reaction, the yellowish transparent liquid containing CD was transferred into a dialysis bag with a molecular cutoff of 3.5 kDa. Dialysis was conducted for a duration of 3 days to remove any lingering unreacted chemical reagents. Obtained carbon quantum dots CD with surface amino functionalization [19].

As shown in Fig. 1b, sodium alginate (SA) was dissolved by stirring at 60 °C for 1.5 h to prepare a 2 wt% SA aqueous solution. Synthesized VSNP and CD were subsequently introduced into the SA solution. Subjected to magnetic stirring at room temperature for a duration of 5 h, a homogeneous solution comprising SA/VSNP/CD was obtained. The spinning solution was extruded through a nozzle with a diameter of 0.7 mm into a coagulation bath containing 3% mass fraction of CaCl2 at a rate of 10 mm/min, controlled by a variable frequency stepper motor. After 5 min incubation period, the spinning solution underwent solidification, leading to the formation of hydrogel fibers through an ion exchange reaction between Na+ in the spinning solution and Ca2+ in the coagulation bath. Following extraction from the coagulation bath, the fibers underwent a series of processes including washing, stretching, and drying, resulting in the acquisition of CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers characterized by a hybrid cross-linking structure.

The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR), manufactured by PerkinElmer, was employed to characterize the functional groups of the samples. The Lambda 950 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer) was utilized for the characterization of intrinsic fluorescence properties. The F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan) was employed for the characterization of fluorescence properties. The mechanical properties of the fibers were characterized using an electronic single-fiber strength tester produced by the First Textile Equipment Co., Ltd. in Changzhou. The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the fibers were analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) manufactured by JEOL Ltd., Japan. The crystalline properties and thermal stability of the fibers were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and a Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), respectively, both manufactured by Rigaku Corporation, Japan. Contact angle measuring instrument (DSA25) from Krüss Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd.: For measuring the contact angle of the samples. Fluorescence microscope from Zeiss: To observe fluorescent microimages of the fibers. The fluorescence lifetime of the fibers was characterized using the FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer, manufactured by Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK. Elemental content of the fibers was measured and characterized using a K-Alpha XPS instrument produced by Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular structural characteristics of VSNP and optical properties of CD
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra for VTES, VSNP, and CD are illustrated in Fig. 2 a and b. VTES observed the CH stretching vibration peak on Si-OC2H5 at 2886 cm−1, detected the CC stretching vibration peak at 1728 cm−1. Furthermore, the asymmetrical stretching vibration peak of Si–O–C on Si-OC2H5 was identified at 1168 cm−1. In comparison to VTES, the spectrum of VSNP reveals the absence of the CH stretching vibration peak on Si-OC2H5 at 2886 cm−1. Instead, a CC stretching vibration peak emerges at 1643 cm−1, the asymmetric Si–O–C stretching vibration peak at 1168 cm−1 experiences attenuation, concomitant with the emergence of a stretching vibration peak associated with –OH at 3340 cm−1. Complete hydrolysis of VTES was indicated [30]. The CD-IR spectrum reveals distinct stretching vibration peaks at 1557 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 for –NH2 and –CO–NH-, respectively.

The fluorescence characteristics of CD were assessed through UV–vis spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectroscopy, as illustrated in Fig. 2c and d. Based on the comparison in Fig. 2c, CD exhibits distinct fluorescence behavior under natural light and ultraviolet light. In ambient lighting conditions, the CD solution manifests as visibly transparent; however, under UV lamp irradiation, it manifests a discernible blue fluorescence. At the same time, the ultraviolet spectrum reveals two characteristic peaks for CD, located at 240 nm and 360 nm. This arises from the π−π* conjugated transition in the surface amino-functionalized aromatic ring of CD and the n−π* conjugated transition in the carbonyl group [31]. According to Fig. 2d, it is evident that under various excitation wavelengths, CD exhibits characteristic peaks at 450 nm in its emission spectrum. Notably, the peak is maximized when the excitation wavelength is 360 nm, confirming that the characteristic excitation wavelength of CD is 360 nm. These results indicate that CD exhibits excellent fluorescence properties.

The surface and cross-sectional morphology of CA and CA/VSNP/CD fibers were examined using SEM and EDS, and the results are presented in Fig. 3 . In the SEM images, it is evident that in comparison to CA fibers, the fluorescent fibers (with a diameter of 70–80 μm) have numerous "grooves" on the surface, and these grooves are more uniform. This is because the introduction of VSNP and CD increases the intermolecular distance of SA, which facilitates the even diffusion of Ca2+ into the SA. Additionally, the hydrogen bonding interactions of hydroxyl groups on VSNP create a uniform dynamic network structure that inhibits uneven fiber shrinkage, resulting in even grooves on the fiber surface. No considerable particle aggregation is detected either on the fiber surface or in the cross-section, implying a relatively uniform dispersion of fluorescent materials throughout the fibers. The circularity of the fiber cross-section is quantified as 0.43 [32]. Simultaneously, EDS analysis was conducted on the cross-section of the fluorescent fibers, and Fig. 3c1-3c3 depict the spatial arrangement of Ca, Si, and N elements on the cross-sectional profile of the fluorescent fibers. The graph illustrates a uniform distribution of Ca, Si, and N within the fiber, confirming the successful introduction and even distribution of VSNP and the fluorescent material in the system.

The FTIR、XRD and XPS analyses were performed on CA fibers and CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers, and the corresponding spectra are illustrated in Fig. 4 a–c, respectively. Fig. 4a displays, in the spectrum of CA/VSNP/CD fibers, the stretching vibration peak of –OH appears at 3436 cm−1. At 934 cm−1 and 815 cm−1, asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of in-plane Si–OH are observed, indicating that Si–OH has been successfully introduced into the system. Compared to the FTIR spectrum of CA fiber, the absorption peak of hydroxyl (-OH) in CA/VSNP/CD fibers are broader. This phenomenon arises due to the establishment of strong hydrogen bonds between the plentiful –OH and –NH2 groups in both VSNP and CD, and the –OH and -COO- groups within the CA macromolecular chain. Which are retained in the fiber, leading to the broadening of the –OH absorption peak in CA/VSNP/CD fibers. Fig. 4b displays the XRD spectrum of CA/VSNP/CD fibers, showing a wide peak in the range of 10∼20°. This can be attributed to the characteristic lattice spacing of typical nanomaterials [33]. It indicates that CD, after being introduced into the system, still maintains good crystallinity, and its lattice structure remains unaffected. Fig. 4c displays, corresponding peaks for C1s, O1s, and Ca2p appear at 286.1eV, 533.2eV, and 348.4eV, respectively, in both CA and CA/VSNP/CD fibers. Additionally, new peaks at 104.2eV and 402.1eV in the CA/VSNP/CD spectrum correspond to Si2p and N1s, respectively, confirming the successful introduction of VSNP and CD into the system. This validates the chemical structural evolution from CA to CA/VSNP/CD.

Fig. 4d–e presents the Gaussian fitting and sub-peak distribution of the hydroxyl (OH) peaks for CA fiber and CA/VSNP/CD fibers, and the consolidated findings are presented in Table 1 . Infrared spectra in the range of 3000∼3650 cm−1 were subjected to peak deconvolution. The percentage of unbound hydroxyl groups in CA/VSNP/CD fibers increased from 1.67% to 2.24%, as compared to CA fibers. The percentage of intramolecular hydrogen bonding experienced a decline from 55.46% to 38.80%, while the proportion of intermolecular hydrogen bonding witnessed an ascent from 42.87% to 58.96%. The increase in intermolecular hydrogen bonds suggests that –OH on VSNP, –OH and -COO- on CA, and –NH2 on CD form strong hydrogen bonds with each other. Consequently, the binding sites of –OH and -COO- undergo occupancy, resulting in a diminished occurrence of intramolecular hydrogen bonding. The –OH groups on VSNP and CA, which did not participate in hydrogen bonding, contributed to an increased proportion of free hydroxyl groups within the system.

The outcomes pertaining to fluorescence efficacy, fluorescence lifetime, and fluorescence microscopy imaging for SA/VSNP/CD spinning solution and CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers are illustrated in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a–b demonstrate that as the excitation wavelength changes from 310 nm to 365 nm, the emission peak experiences a gradual shift towards longer wavelengths, demonstrating outstanding excitation-dependent emission characteristics. The inserted images b1 and b2 in Fig. 5b represent the fluorescence behavior of the fibers when freshly immersed in water and after soaking for 3 h under ultraviolet light. It can be observed that the prepared fibers maintain stable fluorescence behavior in water. Additionally, under UV light exposure, the sample solution exhibits no fluorescence, confirming that there is no leakage of free CD in the fabricated fibers. Fig. 5c shows that exponential fitting reveals two lifetimes in CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers, τ1 and τ2, with values of 1.3313 ns and 8.4617 ns, respectively. The average fluorescence lifetime of CA/VSNP/CD fibers is 6.5465 ns, indicating the presence of different fluorescent centers within the fibers. In Fig. 5d–f, the fluorescent fibers exhibit a consistently even surface, characterized by a homogeneous dispersion of diameters. They exhibit fluorescence at specific wavelengths, emitting both blue and green fluorescence, featuring a more pronounced blue fluorescence. The uniform dispersion of fluorescent particles throughout the matrix is evident, and the conspicuous luminous features identified within the observed field could be ascribed to non-uniform force distribution occurring during the stretching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the strength, hydrophilicity and thermal properties of CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent fibers not only exhibit high visibility but also demonstrate excellent luminescence efficiency and long lifetime, enabling stable fluorescence emission under various environmental conditions. While extensive research has been conducted on alginate-based fluorescent fibers, yielding a series of achievements, it has often overlooked the traditional drawback of sodium alginate fibers, which is their relatively low mechanical performance. Table 2 presents an amalgamation of recent studies elucidating the mechanical characteristics of fluorescent alginate fibers. The data in the table distinctly demonstrates the superior mechanical performance of the fluorescent fibers synthesized in our experiment compared to alginate fibers reported in other literature. It was found through extensive experimental studies (see the supporting information for details of the steps) that when the concentration of SA is 2 wt%, the carbon dot solution is 5 mL, the content of VSNP is 30 wt%, the spinning rate, nozzle diameter, and the concentration of CaCl2 are 10 mm/min, 0.70 mm and 3 wt%, respectively, and the stretching factor is 1.5 times, CA/VSNP/CD fibers exhibit excellent spinnability and mechanical performance. Therefore, subsequent tests were performed on fibers with optimal properties.

Fig. 6 a showcases the fracture strength and elongation at break characteristics of CA/VSNP/CD fluorescent fibers. The tensile strength and elongation exhibit values of 3.81 cN/dtex and 13.16%, correspondingly. This signifies a substantial improvement, exhibiting a remarkable 95.38% increase in tensile strength and an 82.02% increase in elongation at break when compared to CA fibers. The elevation in tensile strength can be ascribed to a myriad of influencing factors. Fundamentally, the integration of VSNP and CD induces the generation of numerous dynamic hydrogen bonding sites, encompassing the –OH groups on VSNP, –OH and –COOH groups on CA, and –NH2 groups on CD. These sites can undergo continuous disruption and recombination, generating cooperative dynamic energy dissipation and maintaining the overall structure of CA/VSNP/CD fibers. Furthermore, the interlaced dual-network structure, resulting from the cooperative influences of dynamic crosslinking and ionic crosslinking, promotes the transfer and balance of energy, thereby playing a role in augmenting tensile strength. The enhanced elongation at break is attributed to the abundant dynamic hydrogen bonds formed among CA, CD, and VSNP within the fibers. During the stretching of the fibers, these hydrogen bonds can be disrupted, rearranged, and recombined to dissipate energy and suppress stress concentration, leading to an elevation in the elongation at break. Additionally, the dual-network interlocking structure also plays a role in transmitting and equalizing stress during the fiber stretching process.

The water contact angles of CA fibers (left) and CA/VSNP/CD fibers (right) are shown in Fig. 6b. Their average values are 38.6°and 45.4°, respectively. In general, contact angles below 90°indicate hydrophilicity, while angles above 90° indicate hydrophobicity. Therefore, CA/VSNP/CD fibers still exhibit hydrophilicity, but the contact angle values have increased. The vinyl structures possess hydrophobic characteristics, resulting in a decrease in the proportion of hydrophilic groups and consequently a decrease in hydrophilicity. However, the presence of other hydrophilic groups in the system (such as –OH, –COOH, –NH2) allows for the formation of hydrogen bonds between different groups. This includes the dynamic hybrid cross-linking network among CD, VSNP, and SA, and the calcium-sodium ion exchange forming a CA ion-crosslinked network structure. The interpenetrating and interlocking structure between the dual networks regulates surface energy, resulting in a reduction in surface tension. This complex combination, to a certain extent, increases the contact angle, indicating an increase in hydrophobicity on the surface, while overall maintaining hydrophilicity.

Based on the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) results, it can be observed that the change trends of CA/VSNP/CD fibers are generally similar to those of CA fibers, as shown in Fig. 6c–d. In the temperature domain ranging from 0 to 200 °C, the reduction in weight of the fluorescent fibers predominantly stems from the liberation of both free and bound water residing within the fiber matrix. Beyond 200 °C, both types of fibers initiate decomposition. The degradation rate of fluorescent fibers surpasses that of CA fibers to a considerable extent. This may be because at high temperatures, hydrogen bonding weakens, making it easier for the co-components with lost binding sites to degrade. Furthermore, dehydrogenation, decarboxylation, and decarbonization reactions take place within the fibrous matrix, leading to the rupture of glycosidic linkages. During the third stage in the temperature range of 300°C–600 °C, further decomposition of the carbonized products occurs, resulting in an augmentation of the ultimate residual carbon content in the fluorescent fibers. This is likely due to VSNP and CD providing numerous dynamic hydrogen bonding sites, stabilizing the entire system. In high-temperature environments, the molecular chains are less prone to free weight loss, indicating that the introduction of these substances has improved the thermal stability of the seaweed fibers.

In this study, the application of surface amino-functionalized CD as fluorescent functional modifiers was employed, with simultaneous introduction of both CD and VSNP into SA solution. By harnessing the –NH2 of CD, –OH of VSNP, and abundant –OH and –COOH groups in SA, a dynamic hydrogen-bonded cross-linking network was constructed, followed by calcium-sodium ion exchange to form a CA ionically crosslinked network structure. Furthermore, an interpenetrating and interlocking structure was established between the aforementioned dual networks. The synergistic multiple dynamic energy dissipation mechanisms significantly enhance the mechanical performance, leading to the synthesis of highly tough fluorescent alginate fibers CA/VSNP/CD through hybrid cross-linking. The research findings indicate that under the following conditions: SA concentration of 2 wt%, a carbon dot solution of 5 mL, VSNP content of 30 wt%, spinning rate of 10 mm/min, employing a nozzle diameter measuring 0.70 mm, a CaCl2 concentration of 3 wt%, and a stretching ratio set at 1.5, the experimental conditions are specified. This signifies a substantial improvement, exhibiting a remarkable 95.38% increase in tensile strength and an 82.02% increase in elongation at break when compared to CA fibers. Moreover, under specific wavelengths, it has the capability to emit both blue and green fluorescence, featuring a focal point of fluorescence intensity concentrated approximately at 450 nm. The average fluorescence lifetime of the fibers was determined to be 6.5465 ns. Leveraging characterization methodologies including FTIR, XRD, SEM, and EDS, it has been verified that the integration of fluorescent substances and nanoparticles into the system has been accomplished effectively, achieving a uniform distribution within the fibers. This investigation establishes a robust approach for the mass production of fluorescent fibers using the wet spinning technique. It holds potential applications in fields such as fluorescent probes, anti-counterfeiting, and biomedical applications.

Dongnan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Song Tang: Methodology, Investigation. Zijian Bai: Investigation. Jianyu Jiang: Investigation. Yao Wang: Investigation. Yue Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Fengyu Quan: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Research Project of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (grant numbers: LJKZZ20220073), State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fiber and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (grant numbers: KFKT202224).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compscitech.2024.110577.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of reinforcement of sodium alginate functionalized halloysite clay nanotubes on thermo-mechanical properties and biocompatibility of poly (vinyl alcohol) nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kouser, S. Sheik, A. Prabhu, G. Nagaraja, K. Prashantha, J.N. D'souza, M.K. Navada, D. Manasa, Effects of reinforcement of sodium alginate functionalized halloysite clay nanotubes on thermo-mechanical properties and biocompatibility of poly (vinyl alcohol) nanocomposites, Journal of the Mechanical Behavior of Biomedical Materials 118 (2021) 104441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A reusable, separation-free and biodegradable calcium alginate/g-C3N4 microsphere for sustainable photocatalytic wastewater treatment

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ag@ MOF-loaded chitosan nanoparticle and polyvinyl alcohol/sodium alginate/chitosan bilayer dressing for wound healing applications

Lee, Ag@ MOF-loaded chitosan nanoparticle and polyvinyl alcohol/sodium alginate/chitosan bilayer dressing for wound healing applications, International Journal of Biological Macromolecules 175 (2021) 481-494.
